factors.summary: The U.S. government defense budgets are showing mixed signals. While there is evidence of growth, particularly in specific areas like satellites and weapons, there are also uncertainties and potential risks of future budget cuts or changes in investment priorities.

evidence.text: The Bipartisan Budget Act of 2018 raised the spending caps for fiscal years 2018 and 2019, providing defense funding. However, future budget cuts or investment priority changes could impact defense budgets.
evidence.subsection: United States Government Defense Environment Overview

evidence.text: BDS revenues increased by $642 million primarily due to higher revenues from derivative aircraft, satellites and weapons, partially offset by lower revenue for fighters and C-17.

factors.summary: Program execution is facing significant challenges across multiple major programs, including delays and cost overruns, particularly highlighted by the 737 MAX grounding and delays in the 777X program.

evidence.text: During 2018, we recorded additional reach-forward losses of $736 million primarily reflecting higher estimated costs associated with certification, flight testing and change incorporation on aircraft, as well as higher than expected effort to meet customer requirements in order to support delivery of the initial aircraft.

The 737 MAX grounding will continue to have a significant impact on revenues until deliveries resume. [...] While we are targeting late 2020 for first delivery of the 777X, there is significant risk to this schedule resulting from issues in engine design and development, which are delaying first flight until early 2020.

factors.summary: The backlog is mixed, with overall backlog increasing slightly but seeing a decrease in the Commercial Airplanes segment. The increase is driven by new contract awards, while the decrease is due to deliveries outpacing new orders and customer liquidity issues. Additionally, the 737 MAX grounding is affecting the backlog, leading to reduced revenues and operating margins until deliveries resume.

evidence.text: "Total backlog increased from $61,277 million at December 31, 2018 to $63,872 million at June 30, 2019 primarily due to current year contract awards including F/A-18 fighters, P-8A Poseidon and E-7 Airborne Early Warning & Control, partially offset by revenue recognized on contracts awarded in prior years."

"BCA total backlog decreased from $408,140 million as of December 31, 2018 to $390,405 million at June 30, 2019 primarily due to deliveries in excess of new orders and a reduction in backlog related to orders from a customer experiencing liquidity issues."
"The grounding has reduced revenues, operating margins, and cash flows, and will continue to do so until deliveries resume and production rates increase."

factors.summary: Organic revenue growth is mixed, with some segments showing increases while others experience significant declines. The 737 MAX grounding has negatively impacted overall organic growth, particularly affecting Commercial Airplanes revenues.

evidence.text: BDS revenues for the six months ended June 30, 2019 increased by $642 million compared with the same period in 2018, primarily due to higher revenues from derivative aircraft, satellites and weapons, partially offset by lower revenue for fighters and C-17.

The grounding is having a significant adverse impact on our operations and creates significant uncertainty. The grounding has reduced revenues, operating margins, and cash flows, and will continue to do so until deliveries resume and production rates increase.

Revenues for the six months ended June 30, 2019 decreased by $8,972 million compared with the same period in 2018. Commercial Airplanes revenues decreased by $10,353 million driven by lower deliveries and a revenue reduction of $5,610 million that was recorded in the second quarter of 2019 related to estimated potential concessions and other considerations to customers for disruptions and associated delivery delays related to the 737 MAX grounding.

factors.summary: The contract backlog shows mixed trends, with evidence of growth in some areas and stability in others.

evidence.text: Total backlog increased from $61,277 million at December 31, 2018 to $63,872 million at June 30, 2019 primarily due to current year contract awards including F/A-18 fighters, P-8A Poseidon and E-7 Airborne Early Warning & Control, partially offset by revenue recognized on contracts awarded in prior years.
The deferred compensation expense increased by $73 million for the six months ended June 30, 2019 compared with the same period in 2018 primarily driven by changes in broad market conditions.

factors.summary: Demand for the company's products is facing significant headwinds due to the 737 MAX grounding, leading to a deceleration in overall demand, particularly in the Commercial Airplanes segment. The grounding has resulted in multiple legal actions and is expected to continue impacting revenues until deliveries resume.

evidence.text: The grounding is having a significant adverse impact on our operations and creates significant uncertainty. Multiple legal actions have been filed against us as a result of the October 29, 2018 accident of Lion Air Flight 610 and the March 10, 2019 accident of Ethiopian Airlines Flight 302.
The 737 MAX grounding will continue to have a significant impact on revenues until deliveries resume.

factors.summary: Unit volumes are declining due to the suspension of 737 MAX deliveries and reduced production rates, particularly affecting the Commercial Airplanes segment. This decline is expected to continue until deliveries resume and production rates increase.

evidence.text: Deliveries have been suspended until clearance is granted by the appropriate regulatory authorities. The grounding has reduced revenues, operating margins, and cash flows, and will continue to do so until deliveries resume and production rates increase.
Commercial airplane deliveries, including intercompany deliveries, were as follows: [...] The 737 MAX grounding will continue to have a significant impact on revenues until deliveries resume.

factors.summary: Input costs are showing mixed trends, with rising costs due to increased inventory spending and production disruptions, but also appearing stable with no significant changes mentioned in other areas.

evidence.text: Inventories increased by $5.9 billion during the six months ended June 30, 2019 primarily due to the suspension of 737 MAX deliveries, which resulted in higher commercial airplane program inventory as we continue to produce 737 MAX aircraft at a rate of 42 per month.
The deferred compensation expense increased by $73 million for the six months ended June 30, 2019 compared with the same period in 2018 primarily driven by changes in broad market conditions.

factors.summary: Labor costs remain manageable as the company has not faced significant wage inflation or benefit expense pressures. There is no mention of significant issues regarding labor costs, though deferred compensation expenses have increased.

evidence.text: During the six months ended June 30, 2019, we repurchased 6.9 million shares totaling $2.7 billion through our open market share repurchase program. In addition, 0.6 million shares were transferred to us from employees for tax withholdings.
The deferred compensation expense increased by $73 million for the six months ended June 30, 2019 compared with the same period in 2018 primarily driven by changes in broad market conditions.

factors.summary: Margins are under pressure, primarily due to the 737 MAX grounding and associated production disruptions, which have increased costs and reduced operating margins. Additionally, lower earnings in the Commercial Airplanes segment have contributed to the compression of margins.

evidence.text: The resulting impacts increased estimated costs to produce aircraft included in the current accounting quantity by $1,016 million and $1,748 million in the first and second quarters of 2019 and reduced 737 program and overall BCA segment operating margins.

Earnings from operations for the six months ended June 30, 2019 decreased by $6,615 million compared with the same period in 2018, primarily due to lower earnings at BCA and a customer financing impairment of $250 million that was recorded in Unallocated Items, Eliminations and Other.

factors.summary: The company has implemented cost reduction actions primarily due to the 737 MAX grounding, such as reducing the production rate. However, these measures are reactive rather than proactive efficiency initiatives.

evidence.text: On April 5, 2019, we announced plans to reduce the 737 production rate from 52 aircraft per month to 42 per month effective April 15, 2019.
The 737 MAX grounding will continue to have a significant impact on revenues until deliveries resume.

factors.summary: Productivity is mixed across the company's segments. While the Defense, Space & Security and Global Services segments show improvements, the Commercial Airplanes segment faces challenges due to additional software requirements and potential production rate reductions.

evidence.text: Productivity is challenged due to the need to address additional software requirements and potential further production rate reductions.

BDS earnings from operations increased by $689 million, primarily due to lower KC-46A Tanker reach-forward losses and gains on the sale of property. BGS earnings from operations increased by $89 million primarily due to higher revenues.

factors.summary: The company's capital expenditure outlook is expected to remain consistent with prior periods, reflecting a cautious investment approach. The focus on managing through specific issues, such as the 737 MAX, suggests a stable or cautious stance towards capital spending.

evidence.text: We expect capital expenditures in 2019 to be consistent with 2018.
The deferred compensation expense increased by $73 million for the six months ended June 30, 2019 compared with the same period in 2018 primarily driven by changes in broad market conditions.

factors.summary: Shareholder returns are being managed conservatively, with no specific changes mentioned, and share repurchases are currently suspended.

evidence.text: Share repurchases under this plan are currently suspended.
The deferred compensation expense increased by $73 million for the six months ended June 30, 2019 compared with the same period in 2018 primarily driven by changes in broad market conditions.

factors.summary: The company's debt leverage is a concern due to significant borrowing capacity and potential risks from increased debt levels, while other sections indicate stability in debt leverage with no significant changes.

evidence.text: At June 30, 2019, the recorded balance of debt was $19.2 billion, of which $4.4 billion was classified as short-term. Debt, including intercompany loans, attributable to BCC totaled $1.9 billion, of which $0.7 billion was classified as short-term.
The deferred compensation expense increased by $73 million for the six months ended June 30, 2019 compared with the same period in 2018 primarily driven by changes in broad market conditions.

factors.summary: The near-term risks primarily stem from the ongoing impact of the 737 MAX grounding, which poses significant risks to revenues, margins, and cash flows. Additionally, potential delays in the MAX's return to service could further exacerbate these challenges, impacting production and financial stability.

evidence.text: If the timing and conditions surrounding a return to service differ from our assumptions, it could have a material effect on our financial statements.
The 737 MAX grounding will continue to have a significant impact on revenues until deliveries resume.

factors.summary: Free cash flow generation is facing significant challenges, primarily due to the impact of the 737 MAX grounding. This issue has led to a substantial decrease in operating cash flows and increased costs, negatively affecting the company's cash position.

evidence.text: Net cash provided by operating activities was $2.2 billion during the six months ended June 30, 2019, compared with $7.8 billion during the same period in 2018.

The net loss from operations for the six months ended June 30, 2019 was primarily driven by the $5.6 billion charge for estimated potential concessions and other considerations to 737 MAX customers and did not affect cash flows.
Other income/(loss), net increased by $162 million and $122 million during the six and three months ended June 30, 2019, primarily due to non-operating pension expense.

factors.summary: The company faces significant litigation exposure from multiple sources, including legal actions related to the 737 MAX accidents and ongoing issues with U.S. government contracts.

evidence.text: Multiple legal actions have been filed against us as a result of the October 29, 2018 accident of Lion Air Flight 610 and the March 10, 2019 accident of Ethiopian Airlines Flight 302. Further, we are fully cooperating with all ongoing governmental and regulatory investigations and inquiries relating to the accidents and the 737 MAX program.

During the second quarter of 2019, we recorded a charge of $109 million related to ongoing litigation associated with recoverable costs on U.S. government contracts. In the second quarter of 2018, we recorded a charge of $148 million related to the outcome of the Spirit litigation.

factors.summary: Capacity utilization is decreasing across the company, particularly in the Commercial Airplanes segment, due to reduced production rates and the impact of the 737 MAX grounding on deliveries.

evidence.text: On April 5, 2019, we announced plans to reduce the 737 production rate from 52 aircraft per month to 42 per month effective April 15, 2019.
Commercial airplane deliveries, including intercompany deliveries, were as follows: [...] The 737 MAX grounding will continue to have a significant impact on revenues until deliveries resume.

factors.summary: The supply chain is facing strain due to production disruptions related to the 737 MAX grounding and potential production rate reductions. However, another section indicates that supply chain health remains stable with no significant disruptions reported.

evidence.text: Impacts related to the reduced production rate have increased costs to produce aircraft included in the current accounting quantity and reduced 737 program and overall BCA segment operating margins. If we are unable to return the 737 MAX aircraft to service in one or more jurisdictions or begin deliveries to customers in a timely manner, we would incur additional costs and/or further reduce the 737 production rate.
The deferred compensation expense increased by $73 million for the six months ended June 30, 2019 compared with the same period in 2018 primarily driven by changes in broad market conditions.

factors.summary: The company is experiencing an increase in inventory levels, particularly driven by higher commercial airplane program inventory due to the suspension of 737 MAX deliveries. Despite this increase, there are no significant risks associated with inventory management highlighted in the filing.

evidence.text: Inventories increased by $5.9 billion during the six months ended June 30, 2019 primarily due to the suspension of 737 MAX deliveries, which resulted in higher commercial airplane program inventory as we continue to produce 737 MAX aircraft at a rate of 42 per month.
The deferred compensation expense increased by $73 million for the six months ended June 30, 2019 compared with the same period in 2018 primarily driven by changes in broad market conditions.

factors.summary: Quality and safety concerns, particularly related to the 737 MAX program, have led to significant disruptions, including a worldwide grounding of the aircraft. These issues have had a substantial negative impact on the company's revenues, as deliveries remain suspended until the situation is resolved.

evidence.text: On March 13, 2019, the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) issued an order to suspend operations of all 737 MAX aircraft in the U.S. and by U.S. aircraft operators following two fatal 737 MAX accidents. Non-U.S. civil aviation authorities have issued directives to the same effect.
The 737 MAX grounding will continue to have a significant impact on revenues until deliveries resume.

factors.summary: The economic conditions present mixed signals, with significant challenges from the grounding's impact on operations and cash flows, while other areas indicate relative stability in the broader market.

evidence.text: The grounding is having a significant adverse impact on our operations and creates significant uncertainty. The grounding has reduced revenues, operating margins, and cash flows, and will continue to do so until deliveries resume and production rates increase.
The deferred compensation expense increased by $73 million for the six months ended June 30, 2019 compared with the same period in 2018 primarily driven by changes in broad market conditions.
